design_philosophy.core_loop: Infiltrate enemy military base stage-by-stage, eliminating soldiers and special units while collecting secondary weapons (grenades, RPG, flamethrower) to overcome increasingly dangerous enemy compositions. Core tension between limited ammo, invisible time limit, and escalating enemy variety.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (limited secondary weapon ammo) vs. enemy pressure (soldiers, gunners, parachutists, jetpack units, flame throwers). Invisible time limit creates persistent urgency without explicit countdown display.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Elite Green Beret commando infiltrating Soviet military installation to rescue POWs; stealth and close-quarters combat (knife) supplemented by captured Soviet weapons.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Strict difficulty progression that escalates enemy composition and spawning between regions (NES western version is deliberately harder than FDS Japanese version). Unforgiving invisible time limit mechanic forces aggressive play rather than cautious progression. Two-player cooperative mode adds dynamic complexity.

difficulty.lives_system: NES western version starts with 5 lives; Japanese FDS version starts with 3 lives. Extra lives awarded at 30,000 and 70,000 points, then per 70,000 points up to 980,000.
difficulty.continues_system: Japanese FDS version allows up to 3 continues after game over. NES international versions removed continue system entirely.
difficulty.checkpointing: NES western version respawns player at last checkpoint upon death. Japanese FDS version respawns at exact spot where player died (more punitive). Two-player mode features instant respawning.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Hidden level select accessible via controller 2 input combination (A+B+Down + directional on controller 1) at title screen. Difficulty escalates automatically: after completing all 5 stages, game repeats from stage 1 at higher difficulty level.

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon pickup system: defeated enemies drop flamethrower (3 shots), RPG/rocket launcher (4 shots), or hand grenades (3-pack). NES version grants 3 uses per pickup; FDS version grants 1 use per pickup. NES version introduced temporary power-ups: Star mark (invincibility, limited duration) and unlimited ammo pistol (limited duration). Knife is permanent melee weapon.
progression.unlock_mechanism: NES version features two exclusive stages not in arcade: Airport stage (between Missile Base and Harbor, ends with rocket soldiers) and final stage inside enemy base (nuclear missile disarmament). FDS version includes secret tunnel areas accessed by destroying landmines in stages 2, 4, and 5; tunnels contain standard enemies and supply runners with random exit points.
replayability_hooks: Difficulty loop: completing all 5 stages restarts from stage 1 with higher enemy density and more dangerous unit types
Two-player simultaneous cooperative mode changes tactical requirements
Hidden tunnel areas in FDS version with randomized exit points
Score-based progression (extra lives milestone system up to 980,000 points)
Regional version differences create distinct challenge profiles (NES harder than FDS)

world_building_techniques: Stage theming reflects military installation progression: outdoor base → warehouse → harbor → airport → interior headquarters
Enemy unit variety signals escalating threat (basic soldiers → specialist gunners → airborne units → flame throwers)
Objective shift between versions: NES reframes as secret weapon destruction vs. FDS/arcade prisoner rescue (narrative context without explicit delivery)
Stage clear cutscenes display stats; ending cutscene shows rescued POWs salute (NES freezes characters; FDS loops music)
Intro cutscene with background detail improvements in NES version establish Cold War infiltration premise
Two-player character identification: Steve and Ben (Green Beret soldiers) establish operative personas

boss_design.philosophy: Stage-ending encounters with special enemy groups rather than singular boss entities. Final encounter involves using dropped rocket launchers to disarm nuclear missile (NES exclusive). Each stage concludes with thematic unit grouping: ambushing soldiers, flamethrower corps, paratrooper units, rocket soldiers.
notable_bosses: Stage 3 ending: Flamethrower corps (replaced paratrooper unit in NES version)
Stage 4 ending: Rocket soldiers (Airport stage, NES exclusive)
Stage 5 ending: Nuclear missile disarmament sequence using dropped rocket launchers (NES exclusive final stage)

faction_indicators: Green Beret commando faction (US special operations, identified by green uniform and knife/captured weapon loadout)
Soviet Armed Forces faction (soldiers, gunners, specialists, jetpack units, flame throwers, parachutists)
Enemy hierarchy implied by unit types: basic grunts, specialist classes (gunners, combat specialists), aerial units (jetpack, parachute, gyrocopter), heavy weapons (flame thrower)
danger_signals: Invisible time limit creates constant pressure without explicit UI warning
Semi-random enemy spawning from screen edges, higher frequency in NES version signals ambient threat
Supply runners and landmines in tunnel areas indicate supply chain vulnerability
Jetpack soldiers and gyrocopters represent air superiority threat
Flame throwers represent area denial hazard
German Shepherds indicate guard dog patrols
environmental_storytelling: Military base layout progression (outdoor compound → warehouse → harbor → airport → underground headquarters) implies defensive perimeter design
Presence of POWs in initial objective frames moral stakes
Nuclear missile in final stage establishes strategic weapons context for Cold War setting
Supply runner units and captured weapons suggest logistics-focused military infrastructure
Mine fields in tunnel areas indicate security layering

sources_used: tcrf - Rush'n Attack (NES, Famicom Disk System) technical documentation
wikipedia - Rush'n Attack gameplay and port information
